observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to carefully plan the start and target completion dates and the timetable for the procurement of all development projects. These plans should be integrated into the AIP, and the implementation should be closely monitored within the specified completion period.
observations.managementAction: The previously unimplemented projects have been reprogrammed and incorporated into the current year. In addition, 17 out of 18 current Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) have already been successfully implemented, reflecting significant improvement in project planning and execution.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to adhere to the guidelines outlined in Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 6.5 of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that all expenses are disbursed in compliance with accounting regulations and for the intended purposes authorized for the funds.
observations.managementAction: A revised LDRRMFIP incorporating the PPAs funded from the STF has been submitted to ensure compliance with the relevant regulatory requirements. Furthermore, the LDRRMFIP for the current year has been properly formulated to include both current year appropriations and STF balances, reflecting all sources of funding and corresponding programs and activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
observations.managementAction: The LSB has immediately discontinued the use of the SEF for meals and other similar ineligible expenditures upon recognition of the deficiency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. stop the practice of splitting requisitions to circumvent existing laws, rules, and regulations, as this is not allowed and could result in audit disallowance; b. instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to resort to the alternative methods of procurement only in highly exceptional cases, to promote economy and efficiency, and if justified by the conditions as provided for in RA No. 9184 and its 2016 RIRR; c. explain/justify why the procurement transactions mentioned will not be suspended or disallowed in audit for violating pertinent provisions of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular 76-41; and d. henceforth, to strictly adhere to the appropriate requirements of procurement law particularly Section 54 of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: A written justification has already been submitted by the concerned office. According to the District Supervisor, the District Advisory for the Year-End and Performance Review, Evaluation and Planning 2023 was issued only nine days prior to the scheduled date of the activity. Due to the limited time between the advisory and the conduct of the event, the Municipality was unable to initiate public bidding, thus resorted to SVP. For CY 2025, measures were successfully implemented to ensure full compliance with Section 54 of RA No. 9184, specifically in preventing the splitting of contracts. In particular, no contract splitting occurred in the payment for meals served during the District Sub-office’s Year-End Performance Review, Evaluation and Planning, and Christmas Party.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a complete set of Financial Statements, and observe the deadline for the submission of said Financial Statements, in succeeding accounting periods.
observations.managementAction: The CY 2024 Financial Statements were submitted on the deadline.

For CY 2024, the relevant LDRRMF accounting records were reconciled with the utilization report.

The CY 2023 Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF was submitted.
The necessary MOA was submitted, and the fund was liquidated.
Necessary supporting documents were attached to the paid disbursement vouchers.
observations.reason:
Not Implemented

(See related finding in Item 12, Part II of this report) | Copies of perfected contracts were not submitted to the Audit Team within the time prescribed and is not submitted at all during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials concerned to implement the identified development projects within a reasonable time or at the target date of its completion, so as not to hamper the desired socio-economic development of the Municipality, and deprive the constituents from the beneficial use of the project.
observations.managementAction: During CY 2024, the identified PPAs of the 20% DF of CY 2023 were implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended for the Municipal Mayor the following courses of action:

Create an Oversight Committee to: (1) review the current fees and charges imposed by the LGU; (2) compute the appropriation rates for each service rendered by the LGU, and (3) submit to the Municipal Mayor the proposed revisions of the rates and charges;

Require the appointed Chairperson of the Oversight Committee, to recommend to the SB the immediate approval of the proposed revisions of the LGU’s Municipal Revenue Code; and,

Request the Municipal Vice-mayor, as the Presiding Officer of the SB to facilitate the immediate passage of a local revenue ordinance adopting the revised rates on fees and charges of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: An oversight committee was already constituted.
observations.reason: There is difficulty yet in determining the rates because there are still procedures to be undertaken, and the personnel concerned has not yet prioritized said task.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should ensure that seminars and conventions attended is responsive to the organizational result and development outcome, keeping in mind the prudent use of the scarce financial resources of the LGU. Also, before approving the authority to attend any seminar-workshop, the authority and competencies of the sponsoring organization/agency should be accordingly verified.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2024, the municipal officials and employees attended the trainings/seminars embodied in the CapDev, or the revised CapDev.

observations.recommendation: Existence and completeness of some Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) amounting to P202.358 million or 78.74 percent of the reported PPE balance remains indeterminable due to non-observance in the management provisions of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual; thus, affecting the fairness of the presentation of the PPE account and presenting opportunities for employee/ third party fraud which may result in financial loss or unauthorized use of assets.

Non-adherence to the provisions outlined in Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circulars No. 2012-001 and No. 97-002 on the grant and liquidation of cash advances to Special Disbursing Officers led to a staggering unliquidated sum at year's end of ₱15.865 million, posing the risk of loss and misappropriation and veering away the matching principle impacting the fair presentation of the financial statements.

Send demand letters and instruct all accountable officers with outstanding unliquidated cash advances to liquidate their cash advances within 15 days from receipt hereof, or else refund the full amount advanced, otherwise, administrative and criminal prosecution may be sought by management and/or the audit team;
observations.managementAction: Sent demand letters to all accountable officers.

observations.recommendation: The accuracy and completeness of the reported Due to GSIS account amounting to P670,936 could not be attested due to a significant variance noted when compared with the records of the GSIS, going opposite with Sections 6 and 7 of RA 8291 and giving risk to risk of incurring penalties and inconvenience to LGU employees in terms of the bents that may be enjoyed.

Collections of Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) delinquencies were not acted on within the five-year prescriptive period set under Section 270 of R.A. 7160, affecting the fairness of presentation of the RPT and SET Receivables due to the necessary recognition of an impairment loss equal to the uncollectible delinquencies/ foregone income and posing a risk of weakened constituent relations
Comply with the posting requirements necessary for the collection of delinquencies;
observations.managementAction: List of Tax Delinquent was posted

observations.recommendation: Conduct extensive tax campaigns in all of the barangays so that taxpayers are apprised of their rights and obligations incidental to their ownership of properties including consequences for non-payment of RPT/SET.
observations.managementAction: Management attended barangay assembly and house to house visitation.

observations.recommendation:
Likewise, we recommend that management:
Spearhead digitalizing the RPT and SET records management in CY 2024 by preparing adequate budgetary support in this regard, and enjoining the Sangguniang Bayan for its approval;
observations.managementAction: Request for funding was made and granted by Sangguniang Bayan.

observations.recommendation: Tax mapping operations essential for the preparation of the Tax Map Control Roll (TMPR) were not undertaken, disregarding Section 472 (b)(5) of R.A. 7160 and item E (4), Section 2 of the Manual on Real Property Appraisal and Assessment Operations; thus, the Assessment Roll may not be accurate and updated presenting lost income opportunity from unidentified taxable properties, coupled with a cascading impact on the accuracy and completeness of the RPT and SET receivables.

Works accomplished as of year-end on contractor-built infrastructure projects could have been verified and accrued had management required the year-end submission of Statement of Works Accomplished (SWA), hence,  misaligning from Paragraph 2 of IPSAS No. 1 and affecting the fair presentation of the financial statements.

Inadequate disclosures on various financial accounts amounting to P16.104 million draw incongruence on required disclosures under IPSAS No. 17 and 30; thus, fair presentations of these accounts were not achieved
Cash insufficiencies under the Trust Fund amounting to P7,687,907 oppose Section 4.8 of P.D. 1445, exposing the local government to liquidity risk.

NTF- ELCAC road concreting project within the buffer zone of Mt. Malindang Natural Range Park was pursued without an Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC), in contravention with P.D. 1586, giving rise to the risk of environmental
consequences, delays in implementation that would warrant the return of undisbursed funds to the national treasury, and endangering the non-achievement of program outcomes.

In light of future actions, we recommended that management make representations with the DBM requesting the extension for the disbursements of the unutilized funds; otherwise, consider the cancellation of the contract agreement with the contractor and return the undisbursed fund if both the extension and Environmental Compliance Certificate are not secured
observations.managementAction: Management requested letter for extension of disbursement of unutilized funds to DBM.
observations.reason: Funds were fully utilized.

observations.recommendation: The Due to NGA account which totaled P48.578 million as of year-end contained fund transfers absent a copy of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), departing from relevant provisions of COA Circulars No. 94-03 and 2012-001 and baring difficulty in measuring timely implementation of the program and the expedient return of excess funds to the Source Agencies (SA).

Reprioritization and non-formulation of Program of Works in several Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) in the 20% Development Fund, valuing P21.462 million opposed the directions provided under the DILG Local Planning Illustrative Guide and the policy enshrined in P.D. 1445; hence, PPAs were not implemented, and benefits were not enjoyed by the public.

Mitigating measures were not identified in the contingency plans and resource gaps were not linked to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Investment Plan (LDRRMIP), running counter to the rationale of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council- National Security Council Joint Memorandum Circular (NDRRMC-NSC JMC) No. 1, series of 2016, exposing the local government and constituents to catastrophic loss.

The absence of written policies or guidelines on the receipt, distribution, and reporting of donations in-kind does not align with Section 2 of P.D. 1445, putting donated goods at risk of losing accountability and transparency.

The municipal government failed to undertake general revisions of Real Property Assessments (RPA) once every three (3) years as required under the Manual on Real Property Appraisal and Assessment Operations, and RA 7160, thereby depriving the municipal government of the possible increase and the right amount of RPT collections that could have been used to support more local projects.

Accounting and reporting requirements prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-002 were not complied with on the CY 2022 Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), consequently decreasing the utility of the LDRRMF Investment Plan and understating the Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds and Trust Liability-DRRMF accounts by ₱5.058 million each.
Instruct the LDRRMO to specify in the LDRRMFIP the expenditure types and the program start and completion dates;
observations.managementAction: Submitted the LDRRMFIP according to format

observations.recommendation: Instruct the LDRRMO to submit monthly to the Office of the Auditor the Report of Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: Submitted to the COA the monthly reports on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

observations.recommendation: Improvement opportunities in cash management could have been enjoyed by the local government had the functionalities of the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) weAccess been availed of.

Existing practice on the disposal of health care wastes generated from COVID-19 vaccination deviates from certain provisions of Department of Health (DOH) Memorandum No. 2021-0031, thus, presenting health and safety concerns for the health care workers and the general public.

Actions taken by management in the implementation of the National Greening Program (NGP) may not be aligned with the program objectives; thus, the benefits and purposes envisioned by the law may not be met.

The obligation of the local government reported under the Due to LGU account in the amount of ₱56.786 million could not be appropriately valued in the absence of a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) on fund transfers received from The Provincial Government of Misamis Occidental, which was made a requirement under COA Circulars No. 94-03 and 2012-001, consequently presenting the risk of non-occurring obligations and the risk that the funds may be used to purposes other than those intended.

Optimum effectiveness and efficiency of the Executive and Legislative Agenda (ELA) for  CY 2023-2025 would have been achieved had the: (1) ELA planning been held within the locality, and (2) base development targets, timeframes, and measurable outcomes been specified, based on the CDP Process outlined in the DILG Local Planning Illustrative Guide.

Registration fees incurred totaling ₱0.710 million on seminars and conferences coursed through the Misamis Occidental League of Local Planning and Development Coordinators, Inc. (MOLDERS) present irregularity concerns due to the unclear authority of the MOLDERS to conduct such seminars and conferences and setting aside Section 10 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.
Stop the practice of paying registration fees to MOLDERS on LGU- organized seminars and conferences.
observations.managementAction: Ceased engaging MOLDERS in organizing seminars and conferences

observations.recommendation: The Municipality uses the cash basis in recognizing revenue from rental of the municipality’s heavy equipment contrary to International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1 that requires the use of accrual basis of accounting, thus resulted to unrecorded receivables of at least ₱487,353.33 as of December 31, 2021.

The accuracy and reliability of the recognized income from the lease of the heavy equipment could not be ascertained due to the Municipality’s absence of internal control over the lease of heavy equipment and non-observance of the LGU to the pertinent provision of the Revised Municipal Revenue Code, thereby undermining the LGU’s potential to generate higher income to support its priority programs.
Consider hiring of heavy equipment operators so that actual numbers of hours used shall be monitored and recorded accurately and proper equipment handling and maintenance can be imposed unto them;
observations.managementAction: Hired a specific driver for a specific heavy equipment.

observations.recommendation: The registration of the Municipality’s seven (7) serviceable motor vehicles was not renewed with the Land Transportation Office (LTO) in contravention with Section 1 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74, thus exposing the LGU to risk of payments of penalties for late registration and non-indemnification in case of accident or loss.

Require the GSO to have an updated monitoring of all motor vehicles with information on the Certificate of Registration (CR) and Official Receipt (OR) to facilitate the timely renewal of registration with the LTO to avoid penalties for late payment and payment of damages for third party liabilities in case of accidents. Also, ensure that the GSO shall file a copy of the OR/CR.
observations.managementAction: Kept files of CRs and ORs

observations.recommendation: Of the total amount of ₱0.500 million intended for financial assistance for persons belonging to the informal sectors, ₱49,000.00 or 9.8 per cent was released to unqualified beneficiaries contrary to Section 4 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and the Municipal’s SB Resolution No. 26_2020, thereby depriving the qualified beneficiaries to maximum cash assistance, and undermining the state’s objective to aid the most affected individual during the height of the pandemic.
Require the identified ineligible beneficiaries to refund the amount received and distribute the refunded amount proportionately to all other qualified beneficiaries;
observations.managementAction: Identified ineligible beneficiaries and already collected the refundable amount.

observations.recommendation: Purchases of drugs and medicines and medical, dental, laboratory supplies with a total amount of ₱0.533 million were recorded as outright expenses instead of the Inventory account contrary to the provisions under International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 12, thereby casting doubts on the balances presented in the financial statements.

The Municipality paid a total amount of ₱109,375.00 for travel expenses in excess of the amounts authorized under Executive Order No. 77 thus, government funds were not safeguarded against invalid claims and were improperly utilized.

Agricultural Produce for Distribution amounting to ₱0.782 million booked by the Municipality remained dormant contrary to COA Circular 2015-009, thereby cast doubts on the reliability of the reported balance as of year-end.

The Municipality paid Productivity Incentive Benefit (PIB) totaling ₱84,000 despite being excluded from the allowances and benefits covered by Salary Standardization Law of 2016, thus made without legal basis and resulted in illegal disbursement of government funds.

Require the refund of ₱84,000 by the officials and employees who authorized the grant and received the PIB for CY 2018 and ensure that all future bonuses and allowances granted to personnel are duly supported with legal basis to prevent disallowance; and
observations.managementAction: Voluntary salary deduction was done for a full refund

observations.recommendation: Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the payrolls and DV of the said claims.
observations.managementAction: Payroll supporting the claim was retrieved.

observations.recommendation: Honoraria granted to the Municipal Board of Canvassers assigned in the Municipality of Don Victoriano, Misamis Occidental during the May 13, 2019 National and Local Elections were charged against the LGU fund contrary to Section 10 of the Omnibus Election Code, thus LGU funds were not safeguarded against invalid claims.

Purchase Orders were not properly accomplished in accordance with Section B of COA Circular No. 96-010 thus, it cannot be ascertained whether the delivery of materials and office equipment conforms to the terms and conditions of the agreement.
Instruct their canvassers to require the prospective suppliers to indicate the brand names and other detailed item specifications;
observations.managementAction: Canvassers were instructed to indicate the brand names in the requests for quotations

observations.recommendation: Direct the General Services Unit to prepare all the purchase orders entered into by the LGU and accomplish properly the P.O.s including the brand names and other detailed specifications of goods to be procured in order to serve the purpose for which the purchase order was prescribed.
observations.managementAction: Purchase orders are indicated with brand names

observations.recommendation: Documents needed for the conduct of contract review and inspection by the COA Technical Audit Specialist (TAS) were not yet submitted despite repeated request in violation of COA Circular 2009-001 and COA Circular 2012-001 thus, precluding the timely conduct of technical evaluation and inspection of the projects.

We have recommended to the management to enjoin the Municipal Engineer, the BAC and other concerned personnel the immediate submission of the documents to avoid further delay in the conduct of technical review and inspection of the LGU’s projects. Moreover, ensure that documents submitted for contract review shall be complete.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted the documents

observations.recommendation: The municipality’s identified evacuation centers were not fully compliant to Section 5.1.2 of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 122, s. 2018 and Section VI (7) of the DEPED-DSWD-DILG-DOH Joint Circular No. 1, S. 2013, thus safety, health, as well as the comfort of the evacuees may not be reasonably assured during calamity.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant regularly reconcile the cashbook balance with the accounting records so that errors committed can be identified and appropriate adjustments in the records can be effected. (1)

Moreover, we recommended that the 

(i) Municipal Accountant draw journal entry vouchers (JEVs) to take up valid book reconciling items identified in the BRS; and (2) 

(ii) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer prepare and maintain complete BRS and cash records, respectively. (3)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE direct the Inventory Committee to facilitate an all-out one-time cleansing of PPE account. (8) 

We likewise reiterated the 

(i) institution of required course of action for missing properties and reflect the required disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements; (9)

(ii) application of provisions with regard to the valuation of items found at station and include it in the PPE account balance when evidence warrants; and (10)

(iii) maintenance of complete property records by the Property Officer and the reconciliation of the same with the accounting records and the result of the physical inventory count. (11)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that 

(a) Management (i) instruct the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents; and (12) 

(ii) exhaust all means to locate documents pertaining to the dormant balances and consider its write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (13)

(b) Municipal Accountant properly classify transactions in the accounting ledgers based on applicable standards. (14)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended the exhaustion of all possible remedies by the Municipal Accountant to gather all documents and information needed to determine the details/breakdown of dormant amounts to facilitate reversion. (15)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the 

(a) Management continue the practice of ensuring optimal use of DF so that socio-economic projects that are beneficial to the agency’s constituents are timely implemented; (16)

(b) Municipal Accountant properly classify transactions in the accounting ledgers based on applicable standards and check the completeness of supporting documents before effecting payment; and (17) 

(c) concerned offices submit the supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2012-001 and pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184. (18)

We also recommended that the LCE enjoin the BAC for their rigid abidance on the procurement procedures, law, and other issuances/rules and regulations on the public procurement of infrastructure projects by making certain that the 

(a) ABC is formulated showing all items of work and costs’ composition pursuant with the determined format for its calculation; (19) 

(b) Technical Working Group (TWG) thoroughly post-qualify the technical documents submitted by the bidder concerned so that it meets the compliance requirements and conditions of the bid; and (20)

(c) costs for Construction Safety and Health Program and Project Signboard are incorporated in the project’s cost. (21)

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the 

(a) MDRRMO to maximize/monitor their utilization for disaster preparedness, mitigation, and rehabilitation objectives; and (22) 

(b) Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team. (23) 

We further recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to present completely in the Notes to the Financial Statements necessary data for disaster risk reduction and management transactions. (24)
observations.reason: Total procurement, issuance and balance of inventory procured were not presented, thus reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE enjoin the GFPS to 

(i) enhance the GPB and GAD AR by completely filling out the needed qualitative/quantitative aspects of the activities implemented, including data on sex-disaggregation pursuant to MCW, as necessary; (25) 

(ii) fully comply with the provisions of the JMC with respect to the constitution of GFPS in accordance with Item 4.1.A of the said law; and (26) 

(iii) observe the deadline for submitting GPB to the Audit Team. (27)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management continue the good practice of adhering to the applicable rules and regulations in the withholding and remittance of taxes and contributions/other payables due to appropriate agencies. (31) 

In addition, we recommended that Management 

(i) reconcile records with concerned agencies if there are discovered discrepancies in the monthly statement of account/billing so that all amounts withheld are remitted intact and on time; and (32) 

(ii) adopt a solution that will facilitate the timely recording and reporting of transactions. (33)
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Manual General Services Officer Designate to immediately undertake the necessary procedures in the disposal of the subject unserviceable properties.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is still in the process of gathering documents related to disposal of unserviceable properties, pursuant to Section 167 of COA Circular No. 92-386 to avoid further deterioration of the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Management Council to: a. explain/justify the incurrence of expenses charged out of the previous years, unexpended balance of LDRRMF despite the non-inclusion thereof in the LDRRMFIP; and b. comply with the provisions of COA circular No. 2012-002 by specifying the disaster management programs and projects and activities in the original and supplemental LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: The Municipal Disaster Risk Management Council did not provide a reason for the non-compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012. Management has yet to identify priority-related programs, projects, and activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to: a. evaluate operational manpower requirements of the Municipality and develop the Organizational structure and staffing pattern, including the formulation of policies governing the structure/staffing pattern to achieve the desired targets of each department consistent with provisions of Sections 76 & 77 of RA No. 7160; b. review and update the qualifications and competencies of JO/COS workers, to determine the needed personnel in accomplishing the desired targets of various offices; c. review the necessity of engaging the services of JO/COS workers in various offices by engaging in providing specific jobs/tasks to be performed in the job order/contract, requiring quantification of accomplishments and requiring attendance at regular office hours, particularly those who are performing regular clerical works and support services.
observations.managementAction: Implemented relies heavily on the services of JO/COS workers to meet the requirements and is currently reviewing the requirements for JO/COS workers in various offices.
observations.reason: Financial constraints and the significant decrease in the National Tax Allotment for CY 2023 prevented the Municipality to formulate the organizational structure/staffing pattern to achieve the desired targets of each department.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that:

The City Accountant review the records on RPT/SET payments for CYs 2005 to 2022 and prepare adjustments for RPT/SET Receivable for the receipt of collection and take up necessary adjustment on accounts Deferred RPT/SET Income during the distribution of collection for the current year payments of tax dues and advance payments; and

The City Accountant follow the accounting entries related to the setting-up of RPT/SET Receivable, receipt of payment and distribution of collection pursuant to Section 36, Chapter 3 and Section 93, Chapter 5, Volume I, of the MNGAS and monitor the accounts to ensure that these are presented fairly in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

The City Treasurer instruct all the SDOs to maintain cashbooks to record the transactions daily such as the cash advance received, disbursements from the cash advance and the balance of accountability; and

The SDOs reconcile their book balances with the cash on hand daily, foot and close the books at the end of each month and their balance of accountability must be reconciled with the City Accountant’s record at least quarterly, pursuant to Section 6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 28, of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the Inventory Committee to complete the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the City following the guidelines and procedures prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:

Direct the City Accountant to maintain a special account for the 20% DF by maintaining a complete set of subsidiary ledgers under the GF Proper for easy monitoring of receipts and utilizations and to enable the generation of the financial statements which can aid in decision-making and other purposes; and

Direct the City Treasurer to open a separate depository account for the 20% DF or the fund may be deposited together with the funds of the GF proper provided a Subsidiary Ledger is maintained for each account to record the receipts, transfers and expenditures involving said fund, pursuant to Section 313 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:

Submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod the updated Schedule of Market Value (SMV) of Real Properties for the enactment of an ordinance after a public hearing is conducted and conduct the general revision of real property assessment at once; and

Direct the OIC – City Assessor to prepare the updated schedule of market values for the different classes of real property situated in the City and, henceforth conduct of general revision of real property assessment every three years pursuant to the provisions of Sections 212 and 219 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

The CHO furnish the Accounting Office with the list of the remaining vaccine allocations received with corresponding unit cost or price; and

The City Accountant record the remaining vaccines as donations in-kind pursuant to Paragraph 96 of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 23 and COA Circular No. 2020-009.  For vaccines received without costs, the City Accountant shall recognize the donations at their fair value as at the date of receipt, which may be ascertained by reference to an active market, or by appraisal.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City of Victorias seek approval from the DBM to utilize the unexpended balances of the financial assistance received considering that the cutoff period to disburse the funds has already lapsed.

Otherwise, the unutilized funds must be reverted to the National Treasury, pursuant to the guidelines set forth in Item 3.8 of LBC Nos. 140 and 141.

We recommended that the City Accountant and General Services Officer undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the City following the guidelines and procedures prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and/or the City Administrator to:

Seek advice from the COA – ICFAO on alternative actions to be done for Intelligence/Confidential accounts whose liquidation documents could not be located anymore and considering that some responsible persons in the AOMs issued were no longer connected with the City; and

File request for authority to write off dormant accounts of the former City Mayor considering that he is already deceased as well as part of his accounts have been outstanding for 10 years and compliance with the requirements in the NS cannot be undertaken as required, following the procedures under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to prepare the necessary documents for the request for write-off of long outstanding dormant cash advances, following the provisions under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment is devoid of legal basis.

We recommended that the Management of the City of Victorias submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:

a. The City Accountant and General Services Office conduct a complete reconciliation of their records by identifying the discrepancies, determine the causes and prepare necessary adjustments, if any, pursuant to Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and

b. The GSO finalized its Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and submit the same to the Appraisal Committee the disposal of unserviceable properties of the City so that adjustment in the books may be effected accordingly.

We recommended that the City Mayor submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod the updated Schedule of Market Value for enactment of an ordinance after a public hearing is conducted and conduct the general revision of real property assessment at once.

We recommended that the City of Victorias submit specific law, other statutory authority, or rules and regulations of competent authority expressly authorizing such payment of additional benefit to its officials and employees pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013. Otherwise, such payment could be considered wanting of legal basis.

We recommended that the City Mayor expedite the search for a suitable site for the establishment of a sanitary landfill following the minimum criteria provided under Sections 40 and 41 of RA No. 9003 so that the City could achieve a safer and organized system or way of waste management.

We recommended that the City Treasurer/Market Supervisor enforce collection of outstanding electricity bills through the issuance of demand letters. On the other hand, ascertain that all tenants are billed based on their water consumption and not only to preferred few occupants to increase the income and decrease unnecessary expenses at VCC.

We recommended that the City of Victorias submit legal basis for the grant of additional bonus in December 2018 to its City elected officials and employees. Henceforth, ensure that benefits or incentives granted to City officials and employees are expressly provided by law, and other statutory authority and the rules and regulations promulgated by competent authority pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Budget Office, CPDO, City Engineer’s Office and all other offices involved in the planning and execution to have a regular monitoring on the period of project execution and assessment of the rate of progress of implemented projects in order to achieve the desired level of efficiency so that the benefits and services that are expected from these projects could be delivered to the constituents in a timely manner; and
We recommended that the City Accountant prepare, as soon as the amount is returned, the necessary adjustment to book up the reversion of the unutilized PDAF funds.

We recommend that the Accounting Office draw a journal entry voucher for properties which have exceeded their economic life reclassifying the total value of unserviceable properties from the PPE account to Other Assets account pending disposal.

We further recommend that the agency require the General Services Office to record obsolete properties in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for proper disposal and subsequent dropping off from the books of accounts. The agency shall likewise create an Appraisal/Disposal Committee to assess the value of these properties.
We recommend that the city, based on available records demand payment of loans from borrowers/beneficiaries.

We recommend that the LGU exert effort to locate separated/resigned employees and collect outstanding cash advances.  Strictly adhere to the provisions of Memorandum Circular No. 155 of the Office of the President as embodied in COA Circular No. 92-391 dated November 25, 1992.

We recommend that the Local Government should enforce immediate settlement of disallowances and suspensions totaling ₱41,152.52 and ₱1,271,019.84, respectively. Also, the submission of necessary documents to support the disbursements should be implemented.
Henceforth, the City Accountant should ensure that government auditing rules and regulations are strictly followed before processing claims or effecting payments in order to minimize suspensions and disallowances in audit.

We recommend that the Officer In-Charge of the Program diligently collect from beneficiaries all due and past due amortizations.





We further recommend that the management submit the approved feasibility study/program design of the Livelihood Assistance Program.
We recommend that solutions to the afore-cited complex problems/deficiencies be dealt with immediately and complied with including the submission of an explanation/justification when necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. Secure from the Authorized Government Depository Banks needed copies of bank statements and take appropriate or alternative actions to determine balances of bank accounts at the end of each month for the purpose of the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statement. If the bank statements are not available, the management should request for the electronic copies of its transactions; and b. Prepare journal entries to record the valid reconciling items in the books in order to reflect the correct balance of the Cash in Bank account and other accounts affected in the financial statements.
observations.reason: The required reconciliations were not prepared. The compliance to the audit recommendation in “a” above is a precondition to the fulfillment of this audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the following recommendation for the Municipal Mayor to direct: a. The Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all properties owned by the municipality which should include the fixed assets; and prepare and submit the RPCPPE in prescribed format not later than January 31 of each year; b. The Property Officer and Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Cards and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards, respectively; and c. The Municipal Accountant to provide an allowance for depreciation on all depreciable assets owned by the municipality.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee cannot conduct the physical count yet because the members thereof will be attending first appropriate trainings in order for this task to be properly done. For CY 2023, not all of the PPEs were provided with depreciation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MPDC to identify the new location of the Isolation Facility and ensure that all development projects to be funded under 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.
observations.reason: See related finding in Item No. 10, Part II of this report. For CY 2023, the first of the two PPAs was realigned to other development project. However, the second PPA is still not implemented in the current year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and other officers and employees concerned to act immediately on the preparation of the required documents and to undertake other procedures for the immediate reconciliation, utilization or return of the above funds.
observations.reason: Although there was a significant amount returned to various source agencies, the supposed liquidated amount of more than P5M, and the unreconciled balance of P115,201.34 remained in the books. Also, prior years transfers were not yet liquidated/implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following: a. members of the GAD Focal Point System to ensure the compliance with implementation of GAD-related PPAs through proper gender mainstreaming or GPB attribution of GAD fund to the programs, projects and activities of the LGU, to fully address the gender sensitive issues in the locality; b. request from the Sangguniang Bayan to formulate and pass a local GAD code or ordinances with the assistance of the LGU GFPS, to support the localization of Magna Carta of Women and to ensure that GAD is mainstreamed in local policy-making and integrated in local development plans.
observations.reason: No document was submitted to prove compliance with this audit recommendation. For CY 2023, the GPB was submitted only during the exit conference, while the GAD Accomplishment Report was not furnished to the audit team as of even date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor the following courses of action: a. Create an Oversight Committee to: (1) review the current fees and charges imposed by the LGU; (2) compute the provincial appropriation rates for each service rendered by the LGU; and (3) submit to the Municipal Mayor the proposed revisions of the rates and charges; b. Require the appointed Chairperson of the Oversight Committee to recommend to the SB the immediate approval of the proposed revisions of the LGU’s Municipal Revenue Code; and, c. Request the Municipal Vice-mayor, as the Presiding Officer of the SB to facilitate the immediate passage of a local revenue ordinance adopting the revised rates on fees and charges of the LGU.
observations.reason: A review of the current fees and charges cannot be performed yet because the required prior attendance of personnel concerned to a training for the purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should ensure that seminars and conventions attended is responsive to the organizational result and development outcome, keeping in mind the prudent use of the scarce financial resources of the LGU. Also, before approving the authority to attend any seminar-workshop, the authority and competencies of the sponsoring organization/agency should be accordingly verified.
observations.reason: For CY 2023, municipal officials and employees still attended trainings/seminars not embodied in the CapDev, or without updating or making a revised CapDev.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following: a) The General Services Officer to maintain stock cards, and submit the Summary of Stock Cards, ICS, and SSMI, together with the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS), to the Accounting Office for recognition of expense of issued/distributed inventories; and b) xxx
observations.reason: The designated Supply Officer still do not maintain Stock Cards, ICS, and SSMI, and the LGU do not utilize the RIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the required reconciliations and to disclose either on the face of the Statement of Cash Flows/ SCBAA or in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with the provisions of IPSAS 2 and IPSAS 24 to achieve transparency in financial reporting.
observations.reason: For CY 2023, subject reconciliations were still not prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management formulate an action plan to implement the audit recommendations, and submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI form to the audit team; copy furnished the DBM, the House Committee on Appropriation and the Senate Committee on Finance, as mandated by Section 93 of RA 10964.
observations.reason: The AAPSI was not accomplished.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant continue to regularly reconcile their cash records to totally zero the gaps, as mentioned. We also reiterated that the Municipal Treasurer prepare complete cashbook.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require (a) the Inventory Committee to carry out complete inventory of PPE, report it on RPCPPE and thereafter, submit a copy to COA; (b) all property records for all PPE  to be maintained by the GSO Designate; (c) reconciliation of all records and inventory report by the GSO Designate and Accounting Office for the eventual cleansing of account; (d) the adherence with Local Roads Asset Management System so that all vital data on roads and its road components for every road network are reflected in pertinent reports and records including the disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the total road network system; (e) the GSO Designate to renew and update the LTO registration of motor vehicles, as warranted; and (f) the Municipal Accountant to comply with the format of CIPLC and correct the overstatement noted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant assure the submission of DVs for CA for confidential activities/projects with complete required supporting documents within seven (7) working days from the release of the check to the COA ATL concerned in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL – For the COA ATL Only” and with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the submissions.

We reiterated our recommendation that the LCE require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents and exhaust all means to locate documents pertaining to the dormant balances and consider its write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Management revert the dormant balances supported by:
Preparing a schedule of the account certified by the Accountant and approved by the LCE;
Providing Certification by Legal Officer that there are no pending cases relative to the account; and
Providing Certification by the responsible officials to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim.

We recommended that the LCE direct the (a) BAC and Accounting Office to submit the lacking supporting documents, as discussed, and ensure the completeness of documentation before the processing of payment of claim/s by the contractor/s; (b) Engineering Office to (i) enjoin the infrastructure project contractors to post the tarpaulin signboards with sufficient details with the necessary update thereof of project status; and (ii) cautiously design PPAs to prevent the occurrence of IUEEU expenditure; and (c) Municipal Accountant to adjust in the books the misstatements noted by preparing the corresponding adjusting entries.

We commended the MDRRM Council for substantially utilizing the LDRRMF. However, we reiterated our recommendation that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) present completely in the Notes to the Financial Statements necessary data for disaster risk reduction and management transactions; and (b) submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

Likewise, we recommended that the LCE order the (a) MDRRMO to disburse the fund only for purposes which are in accordance with the law; and (b) Municipal Accountant to rectify the determined entry errors in the accounting records.

We recommended that the LCE enjoin the GFPS to (i) pursue the implementation of well-planned targets in the GPB so that the gender issues indicated therein could be fully addressed; (ii) supply the COA of the acknowledgment receipt by the concerned DILG Office of the adjusted GPB; (iii) sex-disaggregate/quantify the targets, as necessary; (iv) constitute the GFPS and M&E Team in keeping with Items 4.1.A.2 and 5.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and (v) submit the GPB and GAD AR within the period set in COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that the LCE direct the (a) MSWMB (i) to include a representative from the recycling industry as a member thereof; (ii) that hereafter, the assistance of technical personnel, as enumerated, should be sought for their SWMP and ascertain its regular review and update; (iii) to factor in the establishment of monitoring and evaluation team and prepare accomplishment and progress reports commensurate with the SWMP and (iv) to intensify the SWMP’s implementation through revisit of the Municipal Ordinance No. 2007-81 for its necessary amendments and full utilization of the IEC fund; and (b) MENRO and BAC to submit the lacking documentary requirements.

We recommended that the LCE (i) enforce the authority of the Municipal Treasurer to examine the books of businesses to ascertain the correctness of assessment and collection of business tax; and (ii) instruct the MTO to require applicants of business permits to submit the applicable documentary requirements and keep the documents on file for reference.

We commended the Municipality of Victoria for its relentless support for public basic education and for observing the guidelines in the JC. However, we recommended that disbursements made out of SEF be supported by complete documentation and comply with the submission of documents found lacking.

We commended the Municipality for its pursuance to substantially comply with the withholding and remittance requirements of the BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PHIC.

Nevertheless, we further reiterated our recommendation that the LCE order the (a) Municipal Accountant along with others concerned to exert best effort to facilitate the timely recording and reporting of transactions; and (b) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Human Resource Management Officer (MHRMO) to reconcile records with concerned agencies if there are discovered discrepancies in the monthly SOA/billing so that all amounts withheld are remitted intact and on time.

Moreover, we recommended that the LCE enjoin the MHRMO (a) to automatically deduct from the member’s salary payroll all amortizations for loans; (b) that henceforth, the loan/s to be availed by government employee/s be approved compliant with the general provisions of the GAA with regards to the monthly net take home pay; and (c) to compel those employees who do not have yet their Pag-IBIG MID Number to process such so that their contributions could already be remitted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MPDC and other concerned
officials to carefully plan the start and target completion dates and the timetable for the
procurement of all development projects. These plans should be integrated into the
AIP, and the implementation should be closely monitored within the specified
completion period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO Council to adhere to
the guidelines outlined in Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 6.5
of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that all expenses are disbursed
in compliance with accounting regulations and for the intended purposes authorized
for the funds.

We recommended that the Local School Board stop utilizing SEF for payment of
expenses other than those enumerated under Item 4 of JC No. 01, s. 2017 of DepEd-
DBM-DILG and its Addendum No. 2 under Item 2 of the JC No. 2, s. 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. stop the practice of splitting requisitions to circumvent existing laws, rules, and
regulations, as this is not allowed and could result in audit disallowance;
b. instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to resort to the alternative methods of
procurement only in highly exceptional cases, to promote economy and efficiency,
and if justified by the conditions as provided for in RA No. 9184 and its 2016 RIRR;
c. explain/justify justification why the procurement transactions mentioned will not
be suspended or disallowed in audit for violating pertinent provisions of the RIRR
of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular 76-41; and
d. henceforth, to strictly adhere to the requirements of procurement law particularly
Section 54 of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to henceforth, ensure that fully completed projects funded by the Trust Fund are recognized as credits to the Government Equity account upon transfer to the General Fund.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:
Direct the City Accountant to review the records and make the necessary adjustments to correct the carrying value of affected PPEs; and
Assign a focal person to continuously follow-up the resolution of the noted lapses of the current accounting system used with the COA-GAO; and

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the:
City Accountant to:
a.1	Coordinate with and request the City Engineer to regularly furnish her office with copies of the List of Projects Implemented and their status;
a.2	Prepare adjustments to update the costs of the CIP based on the actual accomplishments or projects completed as of year-end; and
City Engineer to submit the reports on projects implemented together with the necessary supporting documents to the Accounting Office on a regular basis to ensure that all completed projects are properly reclassified to the appropriate PPE accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
City Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to update the CIP accounts based on the List of Projects and its Status Report and other relevant documents submitted by the City Engineer’s Office; and
City Engineer to furnish the City Accountant with the periodic monitoring and status or accomplishment reports of the ongoing projects of the City to ascertain that all on-going projects are properly recorded in the CIP accounts.

We recommended and the City Treasurer and City Accountant agreed to:
Review their records based on the identified discrepancies and immediately record the appropriate adjustments in the books; and
Thereafter, ensure the timely reconciliation of their records so that errors or other adjustments could be identified and effected promptly prior to the closing of the books, in order to reflect correct and reliable Cash – Local Treasury and Cash in Bank balances for all funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
City Accountant to:
a.1	Prepare adjusting entries in the GF books to correct the erroneous accounting entries on the ₱1.440 million purchase of rescue materials and equipment, as follows:
(dr) Accounts Payable			₱1.440 million
(cr) Government Equity			₱1.440 million
a.2	Subsequently ensure that accounting entries for purchases (either equipment or supplies) shall be recorded as deductions to the Trust Liability-DRRMF in the TF books upon incurrence, rather than payment;
a.3	Follow the illustrative accounting entries provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated September 12, 2012 for guidance in the recording of the previous years’ unexpended DRRMF balance; and
The LDRRMO to incorporate the list of projects, programs and activities to be charged against the previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF in the LDRRMFIP under a separate caption, following the guidelines of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to take up the necessary adjusting entries in the books, as follows:
(dr)	Loans Payable			₱174,651.59
(cr)	Government Equity			₱174,651.59

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Review the transactions and balances recorded in the Other Payables subsidiary ledger and identify the proper account classifications;
Prepare the necessary adjusting/correcting entries based on the suggested accounts in the table above along with other errors identified during the review; and
Henceforth, ensure the recording of liabilities under the appropriate accounts pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts and to provide the complete details for each, in consonance with the requirements of IPSAS I in order to establish fair presentation of the account in the FS.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the City Treasurer to instruct all Collecting Officers whose duties permit or require the possession or custody of local funds to apply for fidelity bonds with the Bureau of Treasury and ensure that these are sufficient to cover their accountabilities;
Only allow duly bonded Job Order, Casual and Contractual employees to assist in the collection and disbursement functions; and
Ensure, through the City Budget Officer, that appropriation for the increased bond premiums of the subject Accountable Officers is provided, following the guidelines indicated in Annex C of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
The City Accountant and City Treasurer to immediately remit the unexpended PDAF and unused financial assistance for those affected by typhoon Odette to the National Treasury in compliance with the Supreme Court ruling on PDAF and Item 3.8 of LBC No. 140 dated December 28, 2021 for Odette-funded financial assistance;
For other fund transfers, the City Accountant to coordinate with the Source Agencies (SA) if the unliquidated FTs are also recorded in their books and  inquire as to the proper disposition of the unutilized balances; and
The City Mayor to request authority from the Source Agencies to allow the City to utilize the unspent fund for other beneficial purposes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare and submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation
Statements for all cash in bank accounts, and maintain the required
subsidiary ledgers thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Reconcile the amounts of PPE accounts recorded in the FS and lapsing
schedule;
b. Coordinate with the Inventory Committee to investigate the
whereabouts, condition, and accountability over the assets; and
c. Provide an allowance for depreciation on all depreciable assets or request
for authority from COA to derecognize non-existing/missing PPEs
without available record of accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
a. The Supply Officer and Municipal Accountant to maintain stock cards
and supplies ledger cards, respectively, for issued inventories; and
b. The inventory committee to conduct the semestral physical count of
inventories, and prepare a report thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare a complete set of Financial Statements, and observe
the deadline for the submission of said Financial Statements, in succeeding
accounting periods.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Supply Officer to do
the following:
a. Initiate the requisition of supplies and inventories to be carried in stock
in accordance with COA Circular No. 2002-003;
b. Prepare and maintain RIS, ICS, SSMI, and Property Issue Slip for
issuance of supplies and inventories, whichever is appropriate;

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the heads of office
concerned to observe the prescribed procedures in the requisition of supplies
and inventories provided under COA Circular Nos. 2002-003 and 92-386.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. reconcile the balances of sources of fund between the Utilization Report
and accounting records and reports;
b. submit a correct Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund for
CY 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant and/or other officer and employees concerned to:
a. submit the necessary Memorandum of Agreement for the Livelihood
Assistance;
b. prepare and submit the necessary liquidation report on the utilization of
fund for Livelihood Assistance, and Construction of the National Child
Development Center;
c. immediately implement the identified project funded by the Department
of Social Welfare and Development, if possible, or return the fund;
d. Follow-up with the Department of Agriculture the release of the 10%
intended for Erenas Small Water Impounding Project (SWIP), and
immediately prepare the appropriate liquidation of the fund; and
e. exert efforts to determine the details of the unreconciled amount of
P115,201.34, and prepare the necessary accounting journal entries
therefor.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require the identified persons
responsible or liable in the Notices of Suspension and Notices of
Disallowances to settle the audit suspensions/disallowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials concerned
to implement the identified development projects within a reasonable time
or at the target date of its completion, so as not to hamper the desired socio-
economic development of the Municipality, and deprive the constituents
from the beneficial use of the project.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to optimize the use of its
DRRM funds taking into consideration all the thematic areas as mandated
under Republic Act No. 10121.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that management:
Issue an executive order or internal memorandum identifying and requiring key employees or department heads to submit a timeline in adopting the one-time cleansing of PPE account balance, within 30 days from issuance of such order or memorandum;
Require the municipal accountant and the GSO to maintain separate ledger and property cards and ARE for acquisitions of PPE in C.Y. 2024 and conduct regular reconciliation pending the cleansing of the PPE account.
To expedite the physical inventory taking, issue an internal memorandum requiring each employee to submit every semester a of list of LGU properties under their custody, and let this be a requirement before the payment of any enhancement or productivity bonus.

We recommended that management provide adequate disclosures on the restrictions placed on its land properties and additional information on the realization of its financial assets, specifically the Receivables- Disallowances and Charges and the Accounts Receivable account.

In light of future actions, we recommended that management make representations with the DBM requesting the extension for the disbursements of the unutilized funds; otherwise, consider the cancellation of the contract agreement with the contractor and return the undisbursed fund if both the extension and Environmental Compliance Certificate are not secured.

We reiterated our recommendation that management establish written policy or guidelines on the receipt, distribution, and reporting of donations in-kind. Likewise, we recommend the submission of the List of Donations during the COVID-19 pandemic to enhance transparency and maintain donor confidence in the LGU management.

We recommended that due to the inherent limitation in the general revision of RPT by a component municipality, management should make representations with the Provincial Assessor to fast-track the preparation of the Schedule of Fair Market Values so as not to lengthen or extend departures from the prescribed Appraisal/Assessment Calendar.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the 

(a) Municipal Accountant to (i) establish RPT and SET receivables sourced from the list duly certified by the Treasury Office at the beginning of the year; and (4) 

(ii) adjust in the books the RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred Income accounts which included penalties; (5)

(b) Treasury Office to supply the schedule to the Accounting Office for the drawing of JEV and the set-up of RPT and SET receivables; and (6)

(c) concerned Municipal Officials to strategize suitable effort/s to intensify tax collection. (7)

We recommended that the 

(a) LSB (i) formulate the required three-year program so that utilization of the SEF would be maximized through coordination with the Municipal and Provincial investment and development plans; and (28)

(ii) prepare and approve the SEF budget in accordance with indicative timelines; and (29) 

(b) Accounting Office ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation. (30)

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal personnel to submit all copies of the contracts and/or POs within five working days from their execution, irrespective of the amount, together with all the required supporting documents as mandated under COA Circular 2009001 and ensure compliance with the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS for Local Government Units (LGUs) in the assignment of PO Number.
observations.managementAction: Contracts and/or POs are now submitted to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Assessor prepare and submit to the Office of the Provincial Assessor the updated SFMV or general revision of property assessments as of December 31, 2022, to generate additional income from increased tax assessments. Henceforth, revise SFMVs every three years in compliance with Section 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor has submitted the required SFMV for property assessments to the Office of the Provincial Assessor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit to its servicing bank the requirements for the implementation of an automated payroll system in the payment of the wages of its JO workers to enhance greater control over cash handling and improve administrative efficiency.
observations.managementAction: An automated Teller Machine (ATM) has already been installed in the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant:

Submit all the original copies of the 534 DVs and the supporting documents from January 2022 to December 2022 to the Office of the Auditor; and 

Abide by the existing regulation on the submission of DVs and supporting documents pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular                   No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

The City Accountant conduct timely and regular reconciliation of the City’s depository accounts per books with the balances per bank, resolve any discrepancies and promptly take up appropriate adjustments in the books to present a reliable Cash-in-Bank balance in the financial statements; and

The City Treasurer immediately communicate with the Landbank of the Philippines – Victorias City and request a written explanation relative to the material discrepancies noted on the City’s CIB-LCTD.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant or BAC Secretariat submit all PhilGEPS Registration Certificates or equivalent documents of all hired consultants to the Office of the Audit Team pursuant to Item G.5 of    Annex H of The 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to fast-track the implementation of all remaining BuB projects funded by the National Government Agencies to ensure that the desired benefits/services can be delivered to the City’s constituents on time and address the needs of the poor sectors of the City.

We also recommended and Management likewise agreed that the Project Monitoring Committee of the Local Poverty Reduction Action Team (LPRAT) or designated focal person monitor the implementation of all BuB projects pursuant to the guidelines under DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015.

We recommended that the City Agriculturist conduct inventory of other breeding stocks not yet physically counted and submit inventory reports to support the breeding stocks and other biological assets as of December 31, 2021.

We recommended that the City Accountant ensure the completeness and validity of required documents enumerated under 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated     June 14, 2012 before certifying the disbursements for the transfer of fund to NGO.

We recommend that the City Treasurer conduct massive tax campaign/information as well as improving collection efficiency of RPT/SET Receivables to prevent the accumulation of tax delinquencies. Coordinated and intensified efforts in the implementation of RPT Program should be applied in the city and barangay levels in order to maximize collection of taxes. The City Government should also apply the remedies and sanctions as provided in RA 7160 to deter delayed and non-payment of taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare necessary adjusting entries to record the transfer of the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the Trust Fund, to the General Fund. Corresponding depreciation expenses shall also be provided in the books.
